title: Status Lines, Informational Responses, Content Rules, and Response Parsing · Language Lens: Connect Tunnel Transition
summary: A client parser accepts malformed status codes, stops at 100 Continue, assigns content to HEAD and 204, treats successful CONNECT as ordinary HTTP, and retries after an incomplete response. Planned artifact: Add and verify the connect tunnel transition increment in response control-data and content decision parser. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Transition a successful CONNECT response from HTTP message parsing to opaque tunnel bytes without losing buffered data.
Parse HEAD response metadata as if GET were selected while never consuming response content bytes.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Connect Tunnel Transition · Theory Connect Tunnel Transition
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 6329 in a privacy-sensitive proxy translating between two parser implementations. Build deterministic Go evidence to bound informational responses and transition HEAD, no-content, and CONNECT responses correctly; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then change certificate identity while ALPN stays valid and reject the connection. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Transition a successful CONNECT response from HTTP message parsing to opaque tunnel bytes without losing buffered data.
